paragraphs.body: Anne Lamott describes plot as something that grows out of a character’s actions, and to forward it, a writer must let their characters do and say things that would reveal what the plot is. This has been something that I have tried to allow myself to do throughout the course of the class. Whether it was with my own writing, my classmates’ writing, or the stories that we read in class, I always made sure to look at how the plot was moving along. To me, it is the most important aspect in fiction writing. Without a plot, there is no story. But something that I really got to think about this semester has been how a plot is revealed; everything does not need to be laid out for the reader right at the start of a story. It is okay for there to be some elements of the unknown until whenever the writer is ready to reveal it. A lot of the stories that I’ve read in this class follow this format and I’ve come to really enjoy them.

annotations.comment: Smart! It is really hard to learn unless you make the specific effort to do so.
annotations.context_right: . To me, it is the m

annotations.text: It is okay for there to be some elements of the unknown until whenever the writer is ready to reveal it

paragraphs.body: In The Nanny by Emma Cline, the story opens up at what seems like the end of something much larger. The main character, Kayla, seems to have been exiled to the country. It’s clear to see that she is not there because she wants to be, but because she has to be. Throughout the story we are left wondering what is happening, why she is there, what made her run away. We get bits and pieces through the flashbacks, but the entire story just keeps you questioning. By the end, we are told that Kayla had an affair with the rich celebrity she nannied that got brought to light, forcing her into banishment. The entire story really has you feeling sympathetic for Kayla until that part is revealed, and all of the events following make you go back and reread the to look at the patterns in her behavior. She was not sorry about what she did; if anything she felt like a disservice was done to her and not the other way around. Even the couple she is staying with, friends of her mother who wanted to give her the benefit of the doubt, realize that she doesn’t feel bad about her actions.

annotations.comment: I would use “me” here as it’s not clear that everyone has the same reaction.

paragraphs.body: In Encounters with Unexpected Animals by Bret Anthony Johnston, I was given the same feeling of wondering why the tension in the story was so high, leaving me questioning throughout the piece. My first question stemmed from the opening line; that instantly pulled me in because I wanted to know why he was driving her home. Why him? Why not his wife or the girlfriend’s parents, or even the son? That immediately gave me a very sinister feeling because typically when a story starts off with an older man and a younger woman, there is something very eerie afoot. Even though it didn’t end the way I would have initially thought it would, that mysterious/suspenseful energy carried on throughout the whole piece. This is because of the way she is described by Lambright and the things that he notices; when she is first introduced to us the first thing we learn about is her reputation. Her body. Her tattoo that Lambright notices when she has her hair pulled back. Even if he did not end up doing anything to her, it still seems like an odd way to think about your son’s girlfriend. As I continued to read, I formed my opinion: Lambright isn’t as innocent as the narrator is making him out to be. He knew what he was doing when he missed the turn and dismissed her concerns. He knew what he was doing when he remained silent throughout the drive and he especially knew when he turned off the car in the middle of nowhere, far from their original destination. From all of the descriptions of his son’s relationship, he seems jealous. The way that the dialogue between the two of them drives the story was very enjoyable as well. I liked seeing how in a conversation where Lambright thought he would dominate, he ends up being the one who is left in fear. The same fear he wanted to invoke in her is now invoked in him.

annotations.comment: I like this way of thinking through it — why do I feel this tension?

paragraphs.body: Both of these stories put a spin on reader questions. A reader is supposed to be inquisitive while reading a piece of flash fiction, as there is only a brief amount of time to get through an entire story. But one thing I truly dislike in any type of writing is when I am left with more questions than answers by the end. That is so easy to do with flash fiction, because as said before, there is only a brief amount of time. I like when there is a full resolve, not in the plot or the drama that the characters are facing, but in the story. It is okay if the reader doesn’t get every last inside scoop of the story, but the original drama or plot of rising action/climax of the story should be resolved by the end.

annotations.comment: That’s interesting! I don’t mind questions at the end but I appreciate what you’re saying.

paragraphs.body: I wanted to find a way for my first flash fiction piece to make the readers ask questions but still get the entire story of the conflict they were reading. At first, I spent a lot of time trying to be cryptic with my characters, specifically for Sophia and what exactly happened to her, but I saw that maybe I tried to share too little with the readers. When it came time for revisions, I appreciated that the feedback I got pushed me towards my goal. I realized that the first draft wasn’t really helping the reader get a good picture of what I wanted my story to portray. Reworking it to make the two scenes change places was probably the best thing I could’ve done for my piece. Because the readers were privy to information that was going to be essential by the end, they were left in the dark about other things until the final shoe dropped. What happened to Sophia was revealed within the first few paragraphs, still a lot of the comments that I got from the class talked about how they were questioning why the story was going in the direction that it was.
Note: Not really sure where to go from here or how to clean it up. Any help would be greatly appreciated!

annotations.comment: I think it just lacks a conclusion, to be honest — maybe a goal for you in the future with your writing or something you’re still wondering about?For now,
